perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information in the event and to the extent Recipient reasonably believes any Confidential Information is required to be disclosed by Recipient under the terms of: (i) a valid and effective subpoena; (ii) a statute or regulation binding upon Recipient; (iii) an order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction; or (iv) by a demand or information request from an executive, regulatory or administrative agency or other governmental authority. Recipient shall endeavor to promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such potential disclosure unless recipient reasonably believes that applicable law prohibits Recipient from informing the Disclosing Party of the potential disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information if required to do so by law. Recipient is not required to notify the Disclosing Party of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for the Recipient to 'endeavor to promptly notify' the Disclosing Party. This weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1833(b)
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1833(b) provides whistleblower protection; eliminating the notification requirement could potentially hinder the Disclosing Party from taking action to protect its trade secrets, especially if the disclosure isn't a legitimate whistleblower situation but a simple breach that could have been prevented with proper notification. This creates a tension with the spirit of the DTSA, even if not a direct violation.

Exceptions to prohibitions (a) In General .-This chapter does not prohibit or create a private right of action for- (1) any otherwise lawful activity conducted by a governmental entity of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State; or (2) the disclosure of a trade secret in accordance with subsection (b). (b) Immunity From Liability for Confidential Disclosure of a Trade Secret to the Gov
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Disclosing Party of a potential disclosure of confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could hinder the Disclosing Party from protecting its trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 18 USC 1833, discusses exceptions to prohibitions related to trade secrets. While it doesn't explicitly mandate notification, the omission of notification, as indicated in the Perturbation Explanation, weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to take protective action, potentially undermining the intent of trade secret protection laws. The modification creates a legal loophole, making it non-compliant with the goal of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient understands and agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any person or entity delivering the Confidential Information on behalf of Disclosing Party is making any representation or warrant y, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of any information furnished or to be furnished by it to Recipient (any representation or warranty with respect thereto must be set forth or provided for in a definitive agreement between the parties, if any is entered into in connecti on with the Purpose or certification from Disclosing Party ).
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient understands and agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any person or entity delivering the Confidential Information on behalf of Disclosing Party is making any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy of any information furnished.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the part that allows for future warranty in a definitive agreement between the parties, it limits any possible liability of the disclosing party even if an agreement is made later.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-316 governs the exclusion or modification of warranties. While this clause aims to disclaim warranties upfront, removing the possibility of establishing express warranties in a future agreement directly contradicts the buyer's right to negotiate and receive warranties in such agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-315 Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-317 Cumulation and Conflict of Warranties Express or Implied This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-316 Exclusion or Modification of Warranties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2--202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof. " (3) Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remedy (Sections 2--718 and 2--719).
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the possibility of establishing express warranties in a future agreement, as mentioned in the original text. NY UCC § 2-316, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, governs the exclusion or modification of warranties. The original text allowed for warranties to be set forth in a definitive agreement. By removing this clause, the changed text potentially limits the buyer's right to negotiate and receive warranties, creating a contradiction with the intent of UCC § 2-316, which aims to ensure fair dealing in commercial transactions. The changed text now provides no avenue for setting forth or providing warranties in a definitive agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby further agrees that, in the event of its breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party would suffer irreparable harm and the Disclosing Party’s remedies shall include, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity, equitable remedies such as specific performance and injunct ive relief and shall not be limited to monetary damages. Specifically, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek immediate injunctive relief prohibiting such violation.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient hereby further agrees that, in the event of its breach of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party’s remedies shall be limited to monetary damages.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the possibility of seeking equitable remedies like specific performance or injunctive relief, limiting the Disclosing Party's recourse to only monetary damages. In cases involving confidential information, monetary damages may not be sufficient to address the harm caused by a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 6301 outlines grounds for preliminary injunctions, which are frequently sought in cases involving breaches of confidentiality agreements to prevent further disclosure of sensitive information. By contractually waiving the right to seek injunctive relief, the Disclosing Party is potentially giving up a remedy that a New York court might otherwise grant.

Definitions For the purpose of this subchapter- (1) "United States", when used in a geographical sense means the several States and the District of Columbia; and (2) "employee" means- (A) an employee as defined by section 2105 of this title ; and (B) an individual first employed by the government of the District of Columbia bef
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the Disclosing Party to seek equitable remedies, including injunctive relief, in case of a breach. The changed contract limits remedies to monetary damages, eliminating the option of injunctive relief. The Law Explanation references CPLR § 6301, indicating that preliminary injunctions are often sought in confidentiality breaches. The omission of equitable remedies directly contradicts the potential availability of such remedies under the law, undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent further disclosure of sensitive information through injunctive relief, a remedy a New York court might otherwise grant.
